title: econ-opp-01
scenario: You have a free ticket to Concert A, which you'd value at $50. The same night, Concert B costs $40 and you'd value it at $70. You can only attend one.
prompt: What is the opportunity cost of going to Concert A?

choices.text: $30 — the net benefit of B you gave up ($70 value − $40 price).
choices.rationale: Opportunity cost is the value of the best forgone alternative, net of its own cost.

choices.text: $0 — your ticket to A was free.
choices.rationale: 'Free' ignores the forgone alternative; the cost of A is what you didn't get from B.

choices.text: $40 — the price of the ticket to B.
choices.rationale: The cost isn't B's price alone; it's B's net benefit ($70−$40) that you forgo.

choices.text: $70 — the full value of B.
choices.rationale: You'd have paid $40 for B, so the forgone net benefit is $30, not $70.

explanation: Opportunity cost = the best alternative forgone, valued net of its own cost. Concert A isn't free: choosing it costs you B's net benefit of $30.

title: econ-opp-02
scenario: A lawyer bills $500/hour and can type her own legal briefs twice as fast as her assistant, who is paid $20/hour.
prompt: Should the lawyer type her own briefs?

choices.text: No — her hour is far more valuable spent billing; the assistant has the comparative advantage in typing.
choices.rationale: Comparative advantage depends on opportunity cost, not raw speed: the lawyer's typing hour costs $500 of billing.

choices.text: Yes — she's literally faster at typing, so it's more efficient for her to do it.
choices.rationale: Absolute advantage in typing is irrelevant; her opportunity cost of typing is enormous.

choices.text: Yes — paying the assistant is an extra expense she can avoid.
choices.rationale: Avoiding $20 of wage to lose $500 of billing is a bad trade.

choices.text: It doesn't matter — the briefs get done either way.
choices.rationale: It matters a lot: the allocation of her scarce hour determines total value produced.

explanation: Even with an absolute advantage at both tasks, you specialize where your opportunity cost is lowest. The lawyer's comparative advantage is lawyering; typing is the assistant's.

title: econ-opp-03
scenario: A city owns 10 acres downtown. Used as a park it yields $2M of civic value; the identical land developed as housing would yield $9M.
prompt: What is the economic cost of choosing the park?

choices.text: $9M — the housing value forgone.
choices.rationale: The cost of the park is the best alternative use given up, which is the $9M of housing value.

choices.text: $0 — the city already owns the land.
choices.rationale: Owning it doesn't make it free; the forgone $9M is a real cost.

choices.text: $2M — the value the park produces.
choices.rationale: That's the park's benefit, not its cost.

choices.text: $7M — the difference between the two.
choices.rationale: The net loss is $7M, but the opportunity *cost* of the park is the full $9M forgone.

explanation: Opportunity cost applies even to owned resources: the cost of any use is the most valuable alternative sacrificed — here, $9M of housing.

title: econ-sunk-01
scenario: You prepaid $15 for a movie. Forty minutes in, it's dull. Staying costs you 80 more minutes you'd enjoy more elsewhere; the $15 is non-refundable.
prompt: How should the $15 weigh in your decision to stay or leave?

choices.text: Not at all — it's spent either way, so only the next 80 minutes matter.
choices.rationale: Sunk costs are irrelevant to forward-looking decisions; the ticket price is gone regardless of your choice.

choices.text: It's a strong reason to stay — you paid for it.
choices.rationale: Staying doesn't recover the $15; it only spends 80 more minutes badly.

choices.text: Stay for at least half to 'get your money's worth.'
choices.rationale: There's no money to get back; 'money's worth' is the sunk-cost fallacy.

choices.text: Leave only if you can get a refund.
choices.rationale: The refund is off the table; the decision turns purely on the value of your next 80 minutes.

explanation: A sunk cost can't be recovered by any future action, so it should carry zero weight. Compare only future costs and benefits: 80 minutes here vs. elsewhere.

title: econ-sunk-02
scenario: A startup has spent $2M and 18 months building Feature X. A fresh analysis shows finishing it will cost another $500k, and the finished feature will generate about $300k in value.

choices.text: Stop — the forward cost ($500k) exceeds the forward benefit ($300k); the $2M is gone regardless.
choices.rationale: Only future cash flows matter: spending $500k to earn $300k destroys $200k more.

choices.text: Finish it — abandoning $2M of work would waste the investment.
choices.rationale: The $2M is already spent; 'wasting' it is the sunk-cost fallacy driving good money after bad.

choices.text: Finish it — you're 80% done.
choices.rationale: Percent-complete is about sunk effort, not the forward $500k-for-$300k comparison.

choices.text: Finish it if morale would suffer otherwise.
choices.rationale: Morale is a real but separate factor; on the economics alone, finishing loses another $200k.

explanation: Ignore the $2M — it's sunk. The decision is $500k of future cost for $300k of future benefit, which is a net loss. Kill it.

title: econ-sunk-03
scenario: An airline has spent $100M developing a new route's brand and gates. New data shows operating it will lose $5M/year indefinitely, and there's no resale value in the sunk assets.
prompt: The $100M already spent should change the shutdown decision by how much?

choices.text: Not at all — with no salvage value, the $100M is pure sunk cost; a route losing $5M/year forever should close.
choices.rationale: Irreversible, non-recoverable costs are irrelevant; the forward-looking loss decides it.

choices.text: It justifies keeping the route to avoid writing off $100M.
choices.rationale: The write-off happens regardless of whether you keep flying at a loss.

choices.text: It should be amortized against the yearly loss to see if it 'pays back.'
choices.rationale: There's nothing to pay back; the $100M is gone whether you operate or not.

choices.text: It means you should raise prices until the $100M is recouped.
choices.rationale: If demand supported profitable prices you wouldn't be losing $5M/year; the sunk cost can't be recouped by wishful pricing.

explanation: Sunk and unsalvageable means weight zero. A route that loses money every year with no path to profit should close, no matter how much was spent to build it.

title: econ-real-01
scenario: Your salary rose 3% this year. Over the same year, consumer prices rose 4%.
prompt: Are you better off in what your pay can buy?

choices.text: No — your real wage fell about 1%; prices outran your raise.
choices.rationale: Real change ≈ nominal change − inflation = 3% − 4% = −1%.

choices.text: Yes — a 3% raise is more money than before.
choices.rationale: More nominal dollars that buy less is the money illusion; purchasing power fell.

choices.text: It's unchanged — a raise cancels inflation.
choices.rationale: Only if the raise matched inflation; 3% < 4%, so you lost ground.

choices.text: Can't tell without knowing the dollar amounts.
choices.rationale: Percentages suffice: real ≈ 3% − 4% = −1% regardless of the base.

explanation: Welfare depends on real, not nominal, quantities. A 3% raise against 4% inflation is roughly a 1% pay cut in purchasing power.

title: econ-real-02
scenario: A savings account pays 5% nominal interest this year. Inflation runs 6%.
prompt: What happens to the purchasing power of the money you keep in it?

choices.text: It falls about 1% — the real interest rate is negative (5% − 6%).
choices.rationale: Real return ≈ nominal rate − inflation; here that's −1%.

choices.text: It rises 5% — you earned interest.
choices.rationale: The 5% is nominal; prices rose faster, so real purchasing power fell.

choices.text: It's unchanged — interest offsets inflation.
choices.rationale: Only if the rate matched inflation; 5% < 6% leaves a real loss.

choices.text: It rises 11% — interest plus inflation.
choices.rationale: You don't add inflation to interest; you subtract it to get the real return.

explanation: A positive nominal interest rate can still be a real loss. At 5% nominal and 6% inflation, savers lose about 1% of purchasing power.

title: econ-real-03
scenario: Over 20 years a house's price 'doubled.' Over the same period, the general price level also doubled.
prompt: In real (inflation-adjusted) terms, what happened to the house's value?

choices.text: It roughly held its value — the doubling just tracked overall inflation.
choices.rationale: If the price and the price level both double, the real (relative) price is about unchanged.

choices.text: It doubled in real terms — the price went up 100%.
choices.rationale: That's the nominal gain; adjusting for the same-size inflation leaves ~0 real gain.

choices.text: It quadrupled — price growth compounds with inflation.
choices.rationale: You divide by the price level, not multiply; the real change is ~0.

choices.text: It lost half its value.
choices.rationale: It kept pace with inflation, so it neither gained nor lost much in real terms.

explanation: A nominal doubling that merely matches a doubling of the price level is no real gain. Always deflate nominal growth by inflation before calling it a return.

title: econ-unseen-01
scenario: A city imposes a rent ceiling well below the market rent and it binds.
prompt: What is the most predictable secondary effect, beyond the lower price?

choices.text: A housing shortage — quantity demanded rises while quantity supplied falls.
choices.rationale: Below-equilibrium price raises demand and cuts supply; the gap is a shortage.

choices.text: Everyone who wants an apartment at the capped price now gets one.
choices.rationale: That ignores the supply contraction; fewer units are offered, not more.

choices.text: Landlords simply accept lower profits with no other change.
choices.rationale: Many convert, defer maintenance, or exit — supply falls, the first-order view misses this.

choices.text: Housing quality improves as landlords compete.
choices.rationale: With excess demand, landlords have less incentive to maintain quality, not more.

explanation: A binding price ceiling doesn't just lower price — it contracts supply and creates a shortage, then rations it (queues, connections). The unseen effect dominates.

title: econ-unseen-02
scenario: To make insulin 'affordable,' a government caps its price far below production cost and it binds.
prompt: What's the most likely secondary effect?

choices.text: Shortages and supplier exit — producing below cost isn't sustainable, so quantity supplied drops.
choices.rationale: A price below cost drives suppliers out; the visible low price hides an invisible shortage.

choices.text: Manufacturers absorb the loss and output is unchanged.
choices.rationale: Firms don't produce indefinitely below cost; supply contracts.

choices.text: Demand falls because the low price signals low quality.
choices.rationale: The binding constraint is collapsing supply, not a demand-side quality signal.

choices.text: Everyone who needs insulin now reliably gets it.
choices.rationale: A shortage means the opposite: rationing, gray markets, and unmet need.

explanation: Capping price below cost targets the visible price but triggers the unseen effect: supply dries up. Affordability on paper becomes unavailability in practice.

title: econ-unseen-03
scenario: After a hurricane, a state enforces anti-'price-gouging' rules freezing prices at pre-storm levels for water and fuel.
prompt: What's the most likely effect on getting scarce supplies to those who need them?

choices.text: It gets worse — frozen prices kill the incentive to rush supply in and to ration use, so shortages persist.
choices.rationale: Prices coordinate supply and rationing; freezing them removes both signals when they're needed most.

choices.text: It gets better — low prices guarantee fair access for everyone.
choices.rationale: Low prices with fixed supply produce shortages and queues, not access.

choices.text: No effect — supply after a disaster is fixed regardless of price.
choices.rationale: Higher prices draw in outside supply and curb hoarding; freezing them prevents both.

choices.text: Prices are irrelevant in emergencies.
choices.rationale: Prices matter most under scarcity — they're the mechanism that moves goods to where they're most valued.

explanation: The seen effect is a 'fair' low price; the unseen effect is that no one rushes water in and buyers hoard, deepening the shortage. Prices are a coordination signal, not just a number.

title: econ-tax-01
scenario: A $2/unit tax is legally collected from sellers of a good. Demand is nearly vertical (very inelastic); supply is elastic.
prompt: Who bears most of the $2 tax?

choices.text: Consumers — the inelastic side can't escape, so it eats most of the burden.
choices.rationale: Burden falls on the less elastic side, regardless of who remits the tax.

choices.text: Sellers — the tax is collected from them.
choices.rationale: Statutory incidence (who remits) is not economic incidence (who bears).

choices.text: Split evenly, $1 each.
choices.rationale: Even splits happen only with equal elasticities; here demand is far less elastic.

choices.text: The government, since it designed the tax.
choices.rationale: The tax revenue comes out of buyers' and sellers' surplus, split by elasticity.

explanation: Incidence follows elasticity, not the legal collection point. With inelastic demand and elastic supply, consumers bear most of the tax even though sellers write the check.

title: econ-tax-02
scenario: A payroll tax is legally split 50/50 between employer and employee. Labor supply is much less elastic than labor demand (workers can't easily stop working; firms can more easily cut jobs).
prompt: Who really bears most of the payroll tax?

choices.text: Workers — the inelastic side bears more, regardless of the 50/50 legal split.
choices.rationale: Economic incidence is set by relative elasticity; inelastic labor supply means workers absorb most of it via lower wages.

choices.text: Employers — they're legally responsible for half and set wages.
choices.rationale: Legal responsibility isn't economic burden; wages adjust so workers bear more.

choices.text: Exactly 50/50, matching the statutory split.
choices.rationale: The statutory split rarely equals the economic split; elasticities decide.

choices.text: Neither — the tax is passed entirely to consumers of the firm's product.
choices.rationale: Some may reach consumers, but between the two labor-market sides, the inelastic workers bear most.

explanation: A 50/50 legal split says nothing about who truly pays. Because labor supply is less elastic than demand, workers bear most of a payroll tax through lower take-home wages.

title: econ-tax-03
scenario: In the early 1990s the U.S. taxed luxury yachts, levied on buyers. Yacht demand is very elastic (buyers can spend on other luxuries); yacht supply is relatively inelastic in the short run.
prompt: Who ended up bearing most of the tax?

choices.text: Producers and their workers — the inelastic supply side, as buyers simply bought other luxuries.
choices.rationale: The elastic side escapes; the inelastic side (boatbuilders) absorbs the burden.

choices.text: Wealthy buyers — the tax was charged to them.
choices.rationale: Elastic buyers walked away, so they escaped most of the burden despite remitting it.

choices.text: Split evenly between buyers and builders.
choices.rationale: Very different elasticities produce a lopsided split toward the inelastic builders.

choices.text: The government captured it all as revenue.
choices.rationale: Revenue collapsed precisely because elastic buyers left the market; builders bore the loss.

explanation: The luxury tax is the classic case: elastic buyers dodged it, inelastic boatbuilders (and their laid-off workers) bore it. Incidence lands on whoever can't walk away.

title: econ-trade-01
scenario: Country A runs a persistent trade deficit with Country B — it imports more from B than it exports to B.
prompt: Which country is 'losing' from this trade?

choices.text: Neither necessarily — both gain from voluntary exchange; the deficit is financed by offsetting capital inflows.
choices.rationale: Trade isn't zero-sum; a bilateral deficit reflects saving/investment flows, not a scoreboard loss.

choices.text: Country A — money is leaving to buy imports.
choices.rationale: The mercantilist 'money leaving' framing ignores the goods gained and the capital account offset.

choices.text: Country B — it's giving up real goods for paper.
choices.rationale: B receives assets/claims it values; both sides trade voluntarily because both expect to gain.

choices.text: Whichever has the larger deficit in dollar terms.
choices.rationale: Deficit size isn't a measure of loss; voluntary trade is positive-sum for both.

explanation: A trade deficit is not a loss. Both parties trade because each values what it receives more than what it gives; the deficit is mirrored by a capital-account surplus.

title: econ-trade-02
scenario: Country A is more productive than Country B at making BOTH wheat and cloth (an absolute advantage in each).
prompt: Can both countries still gain by trading?

choices.text: Yes — each specializes where its opportunity cost is lower (comparative advantage), and both end up with more.
choices.rationale: Gains from trade come from comparative, not absolute, advantage — differing opportunity costs suffice.

choices.text: No — A is better at everything, so it should make both and not trade.
choices.rationale: Even a country better at everything gains by concentrating on its lowest-opportunity-cost good.

choices.text: Only if A agrees to a fair-trade subsidy for B.
choices.rationale: No subsidy is needed; differing opportunity costs alone create mutual gains.

choices.text: Only if B can achieve an absolute advantage in something first.
choices.rationale: Absolute advantage is unnecessary; comparative advantage always exists as long as opportunity costs differ.

explanation: This is Ricardo's insight: absolute advantage in everything doesn't preclude gains from trade. As long as opportunity costs differ, specialization by comparative advantage makes both better off.

title: econ-trade-03
scenario: A new machine lets one worker produce what previously took ten workers.
prompt: In the long run, does this necessarily destroy total employment?

choices.text: No — cheaper output and freed labor create demand and jobs elsewhere; total work isn't fixed.
choices.rationale: The lump-of-labor fallacy assumes a fixed quantity of work; productivity gains historically reallocate labor, not eliminate it.

choices.text: Yes — nine of every ten such jobs are gone forever with nothing to replace them.
choices.rationale: That's the lump-of-labor fallacy; freed resources and lower prices generate new demand and roles.

choices.text: Only if the government mandates shorter work weeks.
choices.rationale: No mandate is required; markets reallocate freed labor over time.

choices.text: Yes — automation always reduces the total number of jobs in an economy.
choices.rationale: Two centuries of automation raised living standards without permanent mass unemployment; the total isn't fixed.

explanation: Treating total employment as a fixed pie is the lump-of-labor fallacy. Productivity gains lower prices and free labor, which raises real income and creates work elsewhere.
